title: Teach Me, O Lord
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Teach Me, O Lord - Pastor Tito Lyro - Psalm 119_33-40.txt
outline: Introduction: The He stanza – the little man with arms raised
We are not made for self-reliance; all humans are made to depend on God
3. Make me walk in your commandments (v.35)
4. Incline my heart to your testimonies (v.36)
5. Turn away my eyes from worthless things (v.37)
6. Establish your word to your servant (v.38)
Call to repent of self-reliance and trust in the Lord through Christ
summary: Exposition of the fifth stanza (He, vv. 33–40) of Psalm 119, the sixth sermon in the series. The Psalmist models complete dependence on God through eight petitions covering understanding, obedience, heart inclination, eyes, and reviving. The central truth is that all human beings are made to depend on the Lord, and any attempt at self-reliance is a failure of faith. Listeners are challenged to examine whether the Lord plays merely a part in their lives rather than being the whole of their life, and to repent of self-sufficiency.
transcript:
As we continue our reading of Psalm 119, we are at verse 33. This is our sixth sermon in Psalm 119. We are going to read the fifth stanza of the Psalm — the stanza in which every verse starts with the letter He.

I have nothing clever to say about the letter He, because the American characters for this letter are so different from the original Hebrew character. The Hebrew character was a little man with both arms raised up. That is the idea behind this particular section — coming to the Lord and the Lord meeting him in His grace. So this stanza has the idea of coming to the Lord with arms raised.

## Introduction: Complete Dependence on God

As we read through the Psalm, as we study it, as we memorize it, it is becoming clearer and clearer how the Psalmist — the person who wrote this Psalm, as the representative Christian — relies on the Lord for life itself. For him there is no life apart from the presence of God in his life.

We all rely on people. There is no such thing as a self-sufficient person. We are made to need other people. Every person knows that. A philosopher once asked — I can't remember which one — but the answer is: we are not made to be by ourselves. As much as some of us like to be by ourselves to recharge sometimes, we are not made to not need other people. We are not made for self-reliance.

And above all, above needing other people, all of us were made to depend completely on the Lord. Every last one of us in this room, every last human being who has ever lived, was made to depend on the Lord.

As you listen to this sermon and understand what this stanza means, consider what part the Lord plays in your life. Consider whether the Lord plays merely a part in your life rather than being all of your life. I pray that you will listen with an open heart.

Let me challenge all of us — including myself — to repent from any self-reliance in our lives, from any attempt to live life apart from God. And let us examine ourselves. Are we trusting in the Lord through Christ for all there is to trust?

Paul encourages us in 2 Corinthians 13: "Examine yourselves as to whether you are in the faith. Test yourselves. Do you not know yourselves, that Jesus Christ is in you? — unless indeed you are disqualified."

So listen with an open heart — listen with the attitude that this word is coming from someone who loves you and who is also a sinner and fails like you do — that we might together consider the presence of the Lord in our lives and our reliance on Him.

## The Eight Petitions: All Requests Directed to the Lord

As we consider these eight verses, the first thing I want us to notice is how all the requests the Psalmist makes are directed to the Lord. He directs them all to the Lord because the Lord is the ultimate provider of all these things.

In verse 33, he asks the Lord to teach him His statutes. In verse 34, he asks God to give him understanding. In verse 35, he asks God to make him walk in the path of God's commandments. In verse 36, he asks God to incline his heart to His testimonies. In verse 37, he asks God to turn his eyes away from worthless things. Again in verse 37, he asks God to revive his heart — and brothers and sisters, how much we need that. That reviving is what the old Pietists called the revivification of the Spirit — the coming back to life of our hearts. In verse 38, he asks God to establish His Word to His servant. In verse 39, he asks God to turn away his reproach. And in verse 40, he prays to be revived in God's righteousness.

All these requests are directed to God because He is the one on whom the Psalmist relies. He is the one on whom the Christian relies. All these petitions show the Psalmist's complete reliance on the Lord.

Brothers and sisters, the Christian understands that apart from God's grace, he or she can do nothing as far as obedience goes. Apart from God, we can't do anything. We may try to pull ourselves up by our own bootstraps. We may do something minimally good and pat ourselves on the back. Yet true good is only achieved through total reliance on the Lord.

And that is what our catechisms teach. That is what they mean when they say that our chief aim in life is to glorify God and to enjoy Him. Obedience that flows from complete reliance on the Lord is the ultimate glorifying act — and the primary way that we enjoy God.

Paul tells us that this must be our primary aim. In 2 Corinthians 5:9, Paul says: "Therefore we make it our aim, whether present or absent, to be well pleasing to Him." Paul is talking to the Corinthians who were upset that he was not with them. And Paul says: whether I am with you or am somewhere else, my aim is to please God. That is what I live for. And that is our aim as well.

And Paul says that the love of Christ compels us to do that. In the same chapter, verse 14: "For the love of Christ compels us." This word compel is interesting — scholars aren't sure exactly whether it means to push or to pull, because it can mean either. So let's take both. God pushes us to please Him in every situation. And He pulls us as well. If we were on a rope and God was both pushing us and pulling us toward Him, that is what it means to be compelled by Christ — because He died for us, and in Him we are united to the Father.

All of these requests are pointed to the Lord, because the Psalmist knows that it is because of God that he can do these things. Complete, total reliance on the Lord.

Let me ask you: who are you relying on right now?

## The Commitment to Obey

Secondly, notice the commitment to obey the Word of God in this passage. The Psalmist doesn't simply pray and then wait to be zapped into obedience. He doesn't pray and then forget the prayer and race off until some time when he finally feels like obeying. He commits himself to obedience.

In verse 33: "I shall keep it to the end." In verse 34: "I shall keep your law." Again in verse 34: "I shall observe it with my whole heart." In verse 35: "I delight in it." In verse 38: "I am devoted to fearing you and your judgments." In verse 40: "I long for your precepts."

He didn't just sit there praying and then say, "All right, now I'm going to wait here like a robot for God to zap me." No — he prayed and then committed to doing it.

And this is not a superficial commitment. He is making these commitments to God Himself, who knows his heart. He is not promising these things to you, to me, to his neighbor, to his wife, to his kids. He is speaking to God Himself, who knows exactly what is in his heart — the One to whom we cannot lie.

You know, I think sometimes we hide behind our identity as sinners. We say, "I'm a sinner, so the only thing I can do is sin." We often behave as if we can't help but fall into any temptation that comes our way. And yet the Scripture tells us that is not who we are anymore.

If you don't mind, turn to Ephesians chapter 4, verse 17. The apostle says: "This I say, therefore, and testify in the Lord, that you should no longer walk as the rest of the Gentiles walk" — and he describes what that looks like: "in the futility of their mind, having their understanding darkened, being alienated from the life of God, because of the ignorance that is in them, because of the blindness of their heart, who being past feeling have given themselves over to lewdness, to work all uncleanness with greediness."

Paul said: that is not you. Don't walk that way, because that is not you. You don't have to do these things because that is not who you are. You are in Christ. You have been changed by the Spirit of God. The Spirit of God dwells in you.

He continues in verse 20: "But you have not so learned Christ, if indeed you have heard Him and been taught by Him, as the truth is in Jesus." If you believe in Jesus, this is not who you are.

So brothers and sisters, as much as we fail — that is not a hiding place. That is not where we say, "Oh well, I'm a sinner; that's all I can do is sin," and go on fulfilling the lusts of our flesh. Because all that is doing — to say it in theologically acceptable language — is making excuses for doing what we want to do.

Rather, Paul says: "Put off, concerning your former conduct, the old man which grows corrupt according to the deceitful lusts, and be renewed in the spirit of your mind, and put on the new man which was created according to God, in righteousness and true holiness."

The commitment that the Psalmist had was to do the things he asked God to enable him to do — to obey God. And Paul says: that is what we do, because we are not our former selves. We have left that team. We have taken off that jersey and put on what is consistent with who we are in Jesus Christ.

So brothers and sisters, let's not make excuses. At the very least, be honest and say: "I'm going to sin because I want to. I'm not going to follow God's Word because that is what I choose." Rather than hiding behind theological platitudes that are nothing more than excuses to fulfill our flesh. Let us commit to what the Word of God calls us to do, in obedience to the Lord.

## A Closer Look at the Petitions

Now let's take a closer look at some of the individual commitments in verses 33 through 40.

### 1. Learning from God and Doing What We Learn (vv. 33–34)

The first thing we want to see is that the Psalmist committed himself to learn from God through His Word and to do whatever he learned. Verse 33: "Teach me, O Lord, the way of your statutes, and I shall keep it to the end." Verse 34: "Give me understanding, and I shall keep your law; indeed, I shall observe it with my whole heart."

The Psalmist doesn't see himself as the judge of what is right or wrong. He sees God as the judge of what is right or wrong. He doesn't think he is the one who decides which parts of God's Word are good for him and which parts aren't. He says: "Teach me everything, and I'll follow it all, because You, God, are the best judge of what I should do."

Where are you on this? Are you embracing all that God says? Is your attitude: "God says it, that settles it"? Or are you acting as the judge of what God says — deciding whether what God says is right or wrong, and then going from there?

Who is the ultimate arbiter of what you're going to do? God as He teaches you His Word? Or your own wisdom filtering what God says?

And he commits himself in verse 34 to observe the Word of God with his whole heart. We are often willing to commit partially. We like to keep something back because we are not ready to give all. We will commit partially but not completely.

Are you trying to hold on to any particular sin that is dear to you? "God, I'll give You 98% of my heart, but I'm going to keep this little corner because I really love what's there and I'm not giving it up"? Or are you observing the Word of God with all your heart?

And I mean it — are you listening to this sermon right now while harboring anger, bitterness, or any other sin — making mental excuses for why you don't have to give it up? If so, you are not committing wholly to the Word of God.

### 2. Delighting in the Word and Obedience Itself (v. 35)

The Psalmist also delights in the Word and in obedience itself. In verse 35 he says: "Make me walk in the path of your commandments, for I delight in it." He delights in the Word and in obedience itself — not in some consequence or blessing that may come from it. He believes that the judgments of God, the way God thinks about right and wrong, is good. And because of that he commits to it.

In Psalm 119:72 he says: "The law of your mouth is better to me than thousands of coins of gold and silver."

This man is devoted to fearing God simply for the sake of fearing God. In verse 38 he says: "I am devoted to fearing you."

Are you there with the Psalmist, brother and sister? Is the Word of God good? Do you believe that? That whatever the Word of God says is good? Is God worthy of your commitment to Him regardless of what you get out of it? Is what God says good and right because He is good and right? Or is God your genie in a bottle, where you think you can just rub it and get whatever you want?

Asaph says in Psalm 73:25–26: "Whom have I in heaven but you? And there is nothing on earth that I desire besides you. My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever."

Is that your testimony, brothers and sisters?

### 3. Avoiding Worthless Things (v. 37)

This Psalmist doesn't want to concern himself with worthless things. Verse 37 says: "Turn away my eyes from looking at worthless things and revive me in your way."

This word worthless can mean a range of related things: false, vain (as in the third commandment, taking the Lord's name in vain), futile, deceitful, empty, useless, idolatrous nonsense — things that may not even be sinful but have no value added to the Christian life.

The Psalmist prays: don't let my eyes be focused on worthless things. He wants to come to life in the ways of God. He wants to be revived in the ways of God, not in these worthless, non-value-added things.

I think it is fair to say, brothers and sisters, that we are distracted people. We are distracted by so many worthless things. A football game on Sunday. In my case, TV. Social media. I am a TV junkie — if I could watch TV twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week, I probably would. And I'm owning that to say that I am on the same boat as you at times. We are so easily distracted. Social media, hobbies, the list goes on and on.

And these distractions we are so willing to entertain can choke our faith. In the parable of the soils, the Lord Jesus in giving the explanation in Matthew 13:22 says: "He who received seed among the thorns is he who hears the word, and the cares of this world and the deceitfulness of riches choke the word, and he becomes unfruitful."

A lot of you have read the *Screwtape Letters* by C.S. Lewis, in which Screwtape is the uncle and Wormwood is the nephew. Screwtape tells Wormwood: "The best thing we can do is distract Christians, and they are so easily distracted. If we can keep them distracted, the battle is over."

Brothers and sisters, what is distracting you? What is distracting us from full commitment to the Word of God?

Distraction does to us what the proverbial slowly-boiling pot does to the frog. I don't know if the story is true, but: if you put a frog into boiling water, it jumps out; but if you put it in warm water and slowly turn up the heat, it won't notice and will die in the pot. Is distraction doing that to us?

### 4. Faith Established in God's Word (v. 38)

The Psalmist also prays in verse 38 to be established in God's Word: "Establish your word to your servant who is devoted to fearing you."

This is a similar petition to the prayer of that father in the Gospel of Mark who said: "Lord, I believe; help my unbelief." The word established here carries the idea of confirming something. The Word of God is objectively established. But the prayer is that God would confirm it subjectively to him.

All of us who believe have areas of unbelief remaining in our lives. Every time we sin, we act in unbelief. Every act of disobedience is rooted in unbelief. So we pray with the Psalmist: Lord, confirm Your Word to us that we might fear You — not that something new might be added, but that we would accept what is objectively true as genuinely ours.

This Psalmist is not a superhuman. He is just like you and me — someone whose heart has been changed by the Holy Spirit, who came to faith in Jesus Christ as the Messiah, who is being empowered by the Spirit of God to obey. So this is not an extraordinary experience reserved for someone else. Let us desire it. Let us throw ourselves into this experience.

### 5. Longing for God's Precepts (v. 40)

At the end of the day, the Psalmist is able to pray as he does because he values the Word of God more than the air he breathes. In verse 40 he says: "Behold, I long for your precepts; revive me in your righteousness."

This is a commitment of a heart that has been changed by the Holy Spirit. Brother and sister, this is a commitment of a Christian — not of a superhuman, but our commitment to come to the Lord in total reliance, and then to obey what He says in His Word.
